item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Approximately 22 days before the first day? Huh?

Also, which of these test log entries are supposed to be among the 3% that turn out even weirder due to the infected organ?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Small edit to increase size of 1429 instance.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Since edits are made and I notice the 'crosslink' tab is still on, I will remove it since it has no need for it (as far as I can tell).

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
I'm just gonna clear this up now:

The WikiWalk's team's main goal isn't to link to lesser known articles: It is to restore a wikiwalk, hence the name. Sure, we help with linking to lesser known articles, but making it so people can read a shit ton of stories without having to click off, and just by clicking the links is our main focus.

You know how you can go to wondrous places on wikipedia by wikiwalking without typing anything? That's our main goal right now.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: I think the backlink from 466 here is actually more important given the relative rating difference…

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Oh, I didn't see that. Shit.

I am terrible at my job.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Note the similarities between the 005 instance and 466.

Maybe add a footnote to the 005 instance description along the lines of:

Due phenotypical similarities to SCP-466, genetic material was compared between the two specimens and found to have multiple orthologous sequences, possibly indicating common ancestry.

And add something similar to 466 as a backlink.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Hmm, actually….

How's that?

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: I was thinking more along the lines of hinting they might be the same thing; SCP-466 is a smarter and more specialized parasite that shares a common ancestor.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Ahh, my apologies everyone, I can see where 2828 might not work. I shall edit it to fit SCP-466 instead. Thanks for the feedback.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: What about a link to SCP-466, also an organ that transfigures into an independent organism and escapes the body?

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: 2828 doesn't really work here, because 1429 specifies as large *internal* organ, and 2828 is external. Since skin doesn't seem to be affected by it, it can be presumed that 2828 wouldn't be either. Also, administering it would require seeing it, meaning you can't touch it, or touching it, meaning you can't see it.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Eh, good point.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
What's the point of redacting if you're just going to say what it is

Also, not a fan but not really against it

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer stopped. Edits made.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ show block
– hide block

I propose the following footnote:

1429-B-005 consisted of heart and a majority of the circulatory system. Instance possessed muscular webbing with a high level of articulation in larger veins and arteries. Finer veins and arteries apparently served as sensory organs. Instance was predatory, having developed a small beak within the inferior vena cava and a simple gut. Hunted by ambushing prey, anchoring itself and ripping off pieces of flesh before fleeing.1

Footnotes
1. Due to the phenotypical similarities to SCP-466, genetic material was compared between the two specimens and found to have multiple orthologous sequences, strongly indicating common ancestry. The credibility of this theory is currently unknown.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Love the SCP. Minor contradiction in article:

The time between infection and activation varies greatly, with test subjects displaying incubation times between thirty-eight minutes, and eight months two weeks.

But down below under the test instances, for instance 015 in the nervous system:

13m6d11m

Other than that, love it!

item.title: Staff Post - Reversion Notice
item.content:encoded: Version 15 of this article, which was a reversion to version 13 of this article, has itself been reverted, as it re-added an erroneous extra space.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: its both awesome and sickening at the same time! I'm really glad there wasnt a "brain" "all data redacted" entry, cause those always make me a bit iffy on test logs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I beg to differ.

Nature scares the absolute pee out of me sometimes >.<

Anyway, thank you for the illumination, and the lead. Sundews are neat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A "muscular boneless limb" could be called a tendril, I think. It's not scientifically correct unless referring to plants, but a lot of popular games/movies/books use the word like that all of the time, and I think it inspires the proper imagery, especially for 008.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1 on this article, I expected Alien/The Flood style parasite infections and was pleasantly surprised.

Where was the SCP placed for the last test? The brain is part of the nervous system, I'm wondering if it was tested on the brain, or just the spinal cord and bodily nerves.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I beg to differ.

In other words, no, it's not quite the same as a tentacle — there are things called "tentacles" that are nonmuscular, for instance the motile hairs that sundews use to grab their prey — but it's close enough that the word could be used appropriately.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Mmm, I'm also a little concerned by the boneless muscular limbs. Boneless limbs aren't very good for ambulation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I has a questions.

If it's undetectable by conventional means when it attaches itself to the host organ, how do they know that's what it did?

Is a muscular boneless limb the same as a tentacle?

I also want to see more on where these come from, but that's a statement, not a question.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why do these things only work on humans? The origin sort of implies that they would infect earth animals relatively equally. If it does affect nonhuman animals, why is the Foundation testing it on humans?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The Foundation is not cruel. But it is cold.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As squick-tastic as the gut monsters are, the best/worst part of this is the hosts.
Specifically, how the Foundation gives precisely the square root of zero craps about them.

Imagine, if you will, your eye coming to life and ripping itself out of your face. Scuttling off on crab legs, leaving a trail of blood and leaking eye goop, with your optic nerve dragging behind..
Now lose 1d9999 sanity.
The Foundation reaction? "Non-fatal", in a footnote.

And don't get me started on "..required outside assistance to escape…"
Have a +1. Don't worry, I washed it first.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Adjusted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'll take any recommendation for improvement. I wrote this at my cottage with no Internet or thesauruses handy and just left that bit in because I didn't realize it was incorrect.

Edited. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's basically why I downvoted this. The thing isn't really on a micro scale. I'm in immunology so nits like this really stick in my craw.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Usually, our science folks pounce on things fairly quickly, but isn't a "microbe" by definition too small to see?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well done. One nitpick, though. In 005, you mention that it broke out through the solar plexus. The solar plexus is in reality a web of nerves between the stomach and aorta. In a scientific article, it would be more accurate to say that it exited through the epigastrium, or upper abdomen.

Upvoted for a squicky concept.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The classics are bees.

(Yes, sorry, sorry.)

E: On topic, I quite like this. I keep imagining the microbe as resembling a miniature bacterial Tic-Tac. I particularly enjoyed the eyeball. Up!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I would like to retroactively thank TroyL for the initial inspiration weeks ago. I'm so so sorry for not recalling that you were the one that suggested the idea, Troy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Boy was that creepy. It's like the origins of that one forest full of body parts in South America.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for catching that. I moved a bunch of organs around and must have missed that one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I just liked how "highly carnivorous" sounded. Originally, the -Bs each had their own containment procedures and the intestinal worm was destroyed after killing its handler. That was in the original draft.

I found that the article became way too fucking bloated, even after dividing the -Bs into their own multi-collapsible second page, so that line is just a relic.

I honestly couldn't think of an awesome enough way to pull off the CNS without A) Sounding like TheRaven's Carnivorous Central Nervous System, B) Sounding like BRAINWORM, or C) being boring as fuck. I thought the XXXX-B column for that one was a clever one line "wait hold on wtf" line, which is why I redacted the main Result.

All in all i respect your vote :) thanks for the feedback.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1429-B-006 escaped through the hosts’ mouth. See attached photo.

The attached photo is labeled 1429-B-008.
Aside from that, love it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A lot of the logs were really good. I just find it incomprehensible why you would go and redact the one about the central nervous system. Maybe that's a lack of imagination on my part, but it unfortunately pulled the article out of upvote territory for me.

Good concept all the same.

E: also a couple word choice quibbles. an organism isn't "highly carnivorous", for example. It's either carnivorous or it isn't. It can be a "highly aggressive carnivore" but… well, you get the idea. Little breaks in tone like that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The classics are truly lost, my friend.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I had a draft where it infected a femur, complete with a graphic description of the bone splintering and crawling out from behind the knee. I decided intestinal worm and bellybeasts did that better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: WHOOOSH

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It would be cooler if the skeleton animated but didn't bother 'breaking out.' It just took control of the host person and started walking around. They both fight for control until the host dies from septic shock or something. Then the bones are walking around with a rotting person on them, sort of like a zombie, until the body rots off and then you have a weird skeleton creature.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Intriguing. One wonders how a bio-luminescent microorganism that's so large as to be seen by the naked eye can not be detected by normal means, but otherwise here's a squicky concept. Groovy.

Now, I wonder what if this thing infected, say, a bone. Or the skeleton. In this case, may we assume that the skeleton would, say, POP OUT?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is not a good article to read when you have indigestion…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's fine. No problem :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have to say that this is the exception to the rule that test logs do not make an article. However, I'm not sure that's a positive thing. Have your +1 anyway, because if I find myself actively wondering how many more gruesome permutations are possible, you get your article upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just sorta fell flat for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
eight months two weeks

Are you sure this shouldn't have a "and" or "with" between the months and weeks?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Should the description of test 008 read 1429-006?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As near a perfect SCP as you will see.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Both repaired. Also thank you for the tags.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Little living organs escaping from a human's mouth… That's NASTY, buddy.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No need for sleep tonight. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A couple of minor points stuck out at me:

Collectively, all instances of SCP-1429 are stored at Bio-Site 66 in Facility A-1429.

This is weird language as the first sentence in the Procedures block; due to the way designations are normally assigned, it would be 100% fine to simply say, "All instances of SCP-1429 […]".

SCP-1429 is a large luminescent microbe (~1.5mm in diameter) of […] and dubious origin.

This wording bothers me as well; "dubious" to me usually means that there is an official explanation but it's in doubt; if we simply do not know where it comes from, then we should simply say, "unknown origin".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's like a chestburster except it's literally your chest. +1

Instances are capable of repeatedly infect new hosts

Should be "infecting".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow…squick. I definitely don't wanna meet one of these little guys. Very well done. +1.
